name: Bhatta Kallata
dates_notes: Direct disciple of Vasugupta; flourished in Kashmir under King Avantivarman (855–883 CE), per Kalhaṇa's Rājataraṅgiṇī 5.66 (court-record-grade dating).
core_thesis: Non-Vedāntin comparator (systematic founder of the Spanda lineage). Bhaṭṭa Kallaṭa is the direct disciple of Vasugupta and the figure who, on one important strand of Trika tradition, is the actual author of the *Spanda-Kārikā* — the verse-text that the other strand credits to Vasugupta himself. (The internal-Trika dispute about the *Kārikā*'s authorship is genuine, runs into the school's earliest commentarial layer, and cannot be settled on present evidence.) On either ascription, Kallaṭa is the figure who brings the *Spanda* doctrine into systematic form: in his prose *Spanda-Sarva-Sva* ("the Whole of Spanda") he gives the school's first sustained articulation of *spanda* as the pulsation of consciousness by which manifestation as a whole occurs. *Spanda* is thus the dynamism of *manifestation as a whole* — closer in scope to *prakṛti* itself or to *mahāmāyā* than to any single *guṇa*, and definitively not equatable with *rajas*. Where Utpaladeva's later *Pratyabhijñā* programme will analyse consciousness through the prakāśa–vimarśa structure, Kallaṭa's *Spanda* analyses it through the *unmeṣa–nimeṣa* (unfolding–folding) rhythm that is the school's distinctive contribution.

engaged_works.title: Spanda-Karika
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Authorship disputed within the school's own tradition: one tradition credits the Kārikā to Bhaṭṭa Kallaṭa as a systematic verse-distillation of the Spanda doctrine implicit in his teacher Vasugupta's Śiva-Sūtra; the other tradition (followed by Kṣemarāja in the Spanda-Nirṇaya) credits the Kārikā to Vasugupta himself. Both ascriptions have internal Trika support and cannot be settled on present evidence; the entry honours the dispute by listing the work under both authors with parallel notes.

ascription_evidence.description: Two competing traditions of ascription, both with internal Trika support.
Both ascriptions are accepted as legitimate within different sub-strands of the Trika tradition.

engaged_works.summary: The fifty-three verses of the foundational text of the *Spanda* tradition, organised into four *niṣyandas*. The first establishes the doctrine of *spanda*: consciousness is intrinsically a pulsation (*spanda*) by which the entire manifest universe is continuously projected, sustained, and reabsorbed. *Spanda* is the dynamism of *manifestation as a whole* — closer in scope to *prakṛti* itself or to *mahāmāyā* than to any single *guṇa*, and definitively not equatable with *rajas*. The second *niṣyanda* analyses the operation of *spanda* in the bound subject; the third gives the practical *upāyas* by which awareness aligns with the *spanda*; the fourth gives the condition of full recognition. On the tradition that Kallaṭa is the author, the *Spanda-Kārikā* is the systematic distillation of his teacher Vasugupta's revelatory *Śiva-Sūtra* into verse; on the alternative tradition the *Kārikā* is itself Vasugupta's. The entry conforms to schema by listing the work under both potential authors with full ascription disclosure.

engaged_works.title: Spanda-Sarva-Sva
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored prose commentary on the Spanda-Kārikā; the school's first sustained prose articulation of the Spanda doctrine, irrespective of which way the Spanda-Kārikā authorship question is resolved.

ascription_evidence.description: Modern academic consensus (Dyczkowski editions and studies).

engaged_works.summary: Bhaṭṭa Kallaṭa's prose commentary on the *Spanda-Kārikā* and the school's first sustained articulation of the *Spanda* doctrine in expository form. The *Spanda-Sarva-Sva* — "the Whole of Spanda" — works through each *niṣyanda* of the *Kārikā* with an emphasis on the systematic exposition of *spanda* as the dynamism of *manifestation as a whole*: the *unmeṣa–nimeṣa* (unfolding–folding) rhythm by which consciousness continuously projects, sustains, and reabsorbs the entire experiential field. Kallaṭa is careful to distinguish *spanda* from any *guṇa*: *spanda* is closer in scope to *prakṛti* itself or to *mahāmāyā*, the engine of form rather than one of its modes. The work also gives the school's earliest articulation of the soteriological program of attentive *vimarśa* on the *spanda*-rhythm, which Kṣemarāja will later integrate with the Pratyabhijñā analysis. The Sanskrit prose register is austere and analytic — pre-dating the more ornate commentarial style that Abhinavagupta and Kṣemarāja will later develop — and the work is consequently a key source for reconstructing what the *Spanda* tradition looked like *before* its absorption into the Trika synthesis.

reception_notes: Bhaṭṭa Kallaṭa is the figure who brings the Spanda doctrine into systematic form within the Trika lineage. Kṣemarāja's Spanda-Nirṇaya and Spanda-Sandoha, written in the eleventh century, build directly on Kallaṭa's earlier Spanda-Sarva-Sva, although Kṣemarāja accepts the alternative ascription of the Kārikā to Vasugupta. Mark Dyczkowski's studies are the principal modern treatment of the Spanda tradition and of Kallaṭa's place within it.
